name: Galician (reintegrationist orthography)

ancestors.notes: The same language and the same phonology as standard (RAG) Galician. Reintegrationism is an ORTHOGRAPHIC position, not a different variety: it restores the Galician-Portuguese spelling tradition while the spoken language is unchanged.

orthography_standard.name: Normas ortográficas e morfolóxicas (reintegracionismo)
orthography_standard.notes: The reintegrationist norm, opposed to the official RAG/ILG norm. It spells Galician within the Galician-Portuguese tradition rather than with Castilian conventions.

notes: SAME LANGUAGE, DIFFERENT ORTHOGRAPHY. This spec exists to model the reintegrationist spelling of Galician, not a different speech variety: it inherits its phonology wholesale from `gl` (`allophones_base`) and overrides only the grapheme map. Reintegrationism restores the Galician-Portuguese spelling tradition where the official RAG norm follows Castilian convention. KEY DIVERGENCES from RAG: (1) /ɲ/ is written <nh> (RAG: <ñ>, a Castilian convention) — NOTE the collision: in the RAG norm <nh> is the VELAR nasal /ŋ/ (unha [ˈuŋa]), so the same digraph denotes different phonemes under the two norms, which is precisely why a norm needs its own spec. (2) /ʎ/ is written <lh> (RAG: <ll>). (3) <ç> is restored for /θ/ (RAG has no <ç>). (4) Etymological <j> is restored, pronounced /ʃ/ — Galician has NO /ʒ/, so unlike Portuguese, reintegrationist <j> is voiceless; the spelling is Portuguese-like, the phonology is Galician. Betacism is unchanged (<v> = /b/), as it is a property of the language, not the norm. CONSEQUENCE, and the point of modelling it: reintegrationist Galician is orthographically much CLOSER to Portuguese than the RAG norm is, while being phonologically IDENTICAL to it. Only `spelling_divergence` can see this — `phonological_distance` cannot, by construction.

sources.author: Associaçom Galega da Língua (AGAL)
sources.title: Estudo crítico das Normas ortográficas e morfolóxicas do idioma galego
sources.notes: The foundational reintegrationist critique of the official norm and the statement of the reintegrationist orthography.

sources.author: Regueira, X.L.
sources.title: Galician (Illustrations of the IPA)
sources.publisher: Journal of the International Phonetic Association 26(2)
sources.notes: The phonology inherited here. Confirms Galician has no /ʒ/ — hence reintegrationist <j> is /ʃ/, not the Portuguese /ʒ/.

location.notes: Galicia. This spec is an ORTHOGRAPHY, not a speech variety — it shares its phonology and its territory with `gl`, so it shares its point. [Wikidata Q3908 P625]
